---
title: "HW6"
author: "Group 12 - Shir, Omer & Ido"
date: "26/05/26"
output:
  pdf_document:
    latex_engine: xelatex
    extra_dependencies: ["fvextra"]
  html_document:
    code_folding: show
header-includes:
  - \DefineVerbatimEnvironment{Highlighting}{Verbatim}{breaklines,commandchars=\\\{\}}
editor_options:
  markdown:
    wrap: sentence
---

### Group:

Group members (Name, ID, email)

-   Ido Ravid, 207198177, ido.ravid\@mail.huji.ac.il

-   Omer Sutovsky, 318735107 ,omer.sutovsky\@mail.huji.ac.il

-   Shir Tsarfaty, 207718008, shir.tsarfaty\@mail.huji.ac.il


### Paths and Data


```{r paths_and_data}
# 1. Loading necessary libraries
if (!require('data.table')) { install.packages('data.table'); library('data.table') }
if (!require('splines'))    { install.packages('splines');    library('splines') }

# 2. Sourcing utilities - going up one level from lab6 to StatisticsLab
source("../utils.R")

# 3. File Paths - Updated to match YOUR folder structure exactly
# Note: I used the 'lib2' file since that's what you have in lab6 right now
reads_file  <- "TCGA-13-0723-10B_lib2_all_chr1.forward" 
chr1_file   <- "../lab1/data/chr1_str.rda"

# 4. Global Variables
BIN_SIZE    <- 2500
beg_region  <- 50000000 
end_region  <- 100000000 

# 5. Loading and Binning
if (!file.exists(reads_file)) stop("File not found in lab6 folder!")
chr1_reads <- fread(reads_file, col.names = c("Chr", "Loc", "Length"))
load(chr1_file)

# Creating the dataset using the utility function [cite: 62]
df_main <- bin_data(chr1_reads, chr1, beg_region, end_region, BIN_SIZE)

cat(sprintf("Loaded %d bins | GC range: %.3f-%.3f\n", 
            nrow(df_main), min(df_main$gc), max(df_main$gc)))
```

## Introduction

In this lab we are Building on our previous analysis of the GC-bias, we are now moving to estimate the genomic Copy Number. For this lab, we use a healthy tissue sample as our baseline. In healthy cells, we expect the copy number to be stable and close to 1, allowing us to establish a normal reference before eventually analyzing cancerous samples.

## Q1
```{r}
# Section 1: Function to estimate genomic copy number
estimate_copy_number <- function(reads_vec, gc_vec, fit_model) {
  # Predict expected coverage based on the GC spline model
  f_hat <- predict(fit_model, newdata = data.frame(gc = gc_vec))
  
  # Raw estimate: the ratio between observed and expected
  a_tilde <- reads_vec / f_hat
  
  # Normalize so the median equals 1 (baseline for healthy tissue)
  m_hat <- 1 / median(a_tilde, na.rm = TRUE)
  return(a_tilde * m_hat)
}
```
To extract the copy number, we divide the observed reads by the expected coverage predicted by our GC model. We also apply a normalization constant to ensure the median of our estimates is exactly 1, aligning the data with our healthy baseline.

#Q2
```{r}
# 1. Fit the Spline Model (from Lab 5) 
# We need this model to predict the expected coverage based on GC
library(splines)
mod_gc_sum <- lm(reads ~ bs(gc, knots = quantile(df_main$gc, probs = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75))), data = df_main)

# 2. Select the 1000 consecutive bins
df_sample <- df_main[1:1000, ]

# 3. Estimate Copy Number
# Now 'mod_gc_sum' exists and the function will work
df_sample$a_hat <- estimate_copy_number(df_sample$reads, df_sample$gc, mod_gc_sum)

# 4. Numerical measure: How close are we to 1?
mae_cn <- mean(abs(df_sample$a_hat - 1))
cat(sprintf("Average distance from baseline (1.0): %.4f\n", mae_cn))

# 5. Plotting the results
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
hist(df_sample$a_hat, breaks = 30, col = "gray90", border = "white",
     main = "Marginal Distribution", xlab = "Estimated CN")
abline(v = 1, col = "red", lwd = 2, lty = 2)

plot(df_sample$a_hat, type = "l", col = "steelblue",
     main = "CN across Position", xlab = "Bin Index", ylab = "a_hat")
abline(h = 1, col = "red", lty = 2)
```
2a: Visualizing the Estimates, The plots show that we successfully removed the GC bias. The histogram is centered around 1, which is exactly what we expect for a healthy sample. Looking at the spatial plot, the estimates are stable without any major jumps, confirming that the copy number is uniform across this genomic region.  
2b: Measuring the MAE of about 0.15 means that, on average, each bin deviates from the baseline by 15\%. This deviation represents the residual noise left in our model: a mix of random sampling variation and other minor technical factors.   


## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.
